fact: June 20 is almost always the longest day of the year in the Northern Hemisphere! The sun stays up for over 16 hours in many places.
In Alaska on this date, the sun barely sets at all - it just dips below the horizon for a few hours and pops right back up like a yo-yo!
Down in Antarctica, June 20 is one of the darkest days of winter - some places get less than 4 hours of dim sunlight.
This is when fireflies start their crazy light shows! Male fireflies flash special patterns to attract girlfriends in the dark.
Strawberries are at their absolute juiciest and sweetest around June 20 in most of North America - that's why it's called strawberry season!
Baby deer called fawns are stumbling around on wobbly legs right now, learning to walk while their moms search for food.
The air temperature can swing wildly on June 20 - it might be 60°F at sunrise and 85°F by afternoon in the same spot!
Cicadas are screaming their loudest songs of the year around this time, making noise that can reach 120 decibels - louder than a chainsaw!
Ocean water in the Northern Hemisphere is still pretty chilly on June 20, even though the air feels hot - the water takes longer to warm up than land.
This date falls right between Gemini and Cancer zodiac signs, so people born on June 20 are on the 'cusp' and might act like both signs.
Dandelions have gone completely fluffy and white by now - those aren't flowers anymore, they're thousands of tiny parachute seeds ready to fly!
In northern countries, the sun sets so late that kids can play outside until 9 or 10 PM and it still looks like daytime!
